slides.title: Files: Memory That Survives
slides.body: Variables vanish the moment a program stops. **Files** let your Python program save data to disk — and read it back anytime.
slides.tip: File I/O is how programs keep score, write logs, and store user data between runs.

slides.title: open() Unlocks Any File
slides.body: Pass a **filename** and a **mode** to `open()`. Mode `"r"` reads, `"w"` writes (erasing first!), and `"a"` appends to the end.
slides.code:
f = open("notes.txt", "r")
content = f.read()
f.close()  # easy to forget!
slides.tip: Always specify encoding="utf-8" to keep your code portable across machines.

slides.title: with open() — The Safe Way
slides.body: A `with` block **automatically closes** the file when the block ends — even if your code crashes halfway through. No `f.close()` needed.
slides.code:
with open("notes.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    content = f.read()

print(content)

slides.title: Reading a File's Contents
slides.body: `read()` loads the whole file as one string. To loop line-by-line without loading everything, **iterate directly over the file**.
slides.output:
Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
Python is great,
And so are you.
slides.tip: strip() removes the invisible \n at the end of each line.

slides.title: Writing & Appending
slides.body: Use `"w"` to create or overwrite a file, or `"a"` to safely add lines at the end. `write()` saves text — include `\n` yourself.
slides.code:
with open("log.txt", "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("User logged in\n")
slides.tip: "w" silently erases existing content — use "a" when you want to keep what's already there.

slides.title: You've Got File Power
slides.body: You can now read, write, and append files safely using `with open(...)`. Your programs can **remember things** between runs.
slides.tip: Next time you need to save data, reach for with open(..., "w") to write and with open(..., "r") to read it back.
